title: What is a caravan in law
paras: So — what is a caravan in law? The foundation of every caravan case is section twenty-nine, subsection one, of the Caravan Sites and Control of Development Act 1960. A caravan is a structure designed or adapted for human habitation which is capable of being moved from one place to another — whether by towing, or by transport on a motor vehicle or trailer. Three components flow from that. First, the structure must be designed or adapted for living in. Whether it looks like a house or a hut is irrelevant — which is why containers, converted horseboxes and modular cabins have to be examined on the test, not on their appearance. Second, it must be capable of being moved. And third, it is expressly not a building. Parliament intended caravans to be treated as chattels, unless their installation later amounts to building operations.

"Capable of being moved" is about physical capability, not convenience. The courts — notably in Bunn — established that it means movable without structural damage, by normal methods: towing, lifting, craning, or a low-loader. It doesn't need wheels. It doesn't need to be regularly moved. And lawful movability on the highway is irrelevant — an oversize load needing a special escort still passes. What defeats mobility is physical: brick skirts tied into the structure, anchoring bolts or plates fixed through the chassis, deep concrete bases, or porches and steps integrated so that removal means demolition.

Inspectors now invariably frame the analysis as three tests — size, construction and assembly, and mobility — and every limb must be satisfied. Fail any one, and the structure is not a caravan. It is presumptively a building, its siting becomes operational development, planning permission is required unless a permitted development right applies, fees become floor-area based, and enforcement becomes much easier for the authority. Pass all three, and the unit is a caravan — usually a chattel — and its siting is not development unless the use of the land has materially changed.

Two regulatory regimes overlap here, and they must be kept apart. The Caravan Sites Acts define what a caravan is and run the site-licensing system — safety, spacing, amenity standards. They do not grant planning permission. The Town and Country Planning Act 1990 — the T-C-P-A — regulates development and material changes of use, and it doesn't care what the licence says. A structure can be a caravan for licensing purposes and still involve development in planning. An enforcement notice citing licensing breaches has made a legal misstep. The Acts describe a structure; the T-C-P-A regulates what you do with it.

title: Twin units and the size limits
paras: Now, twin units and the size limits. Section thirteen of the Caravan Sites Act 1968 extends the definition to twin-unit caravans — Parliament's answer to homes too large to move in one piece. Its tests apply in addition to the 1960 Act, and there are three. First, no more than two sections, constructed separately and designed to be assembled on site. Second, joined only by bolts, clamps or other devices — the final act of assembly must be a simple mechanical joining operation, not construction work akin to building a house. And third, physically capable of being moved when assembled — even if highway movement would need a special escort.

The dimensional limits were last amended in 2006: twenty metres in length, excluding the drawbar; six point eight metres in width; and three point zero five metres internal height of the living accommodation. Exceed any one of those and the structure is not a caravan. The consequence is immediate — it becomes a building for planning purposes unless shown otherwise. Even a small breach is fatal: an internal height of three point zero seven metres fails. And the roof void does not count as living accommodation for the height measurement — a point that has decided appeals.

The construction and assembly test does not permit structural welding on site, cutting and reframing walls or floors, construction by traditional building methods, brickwork skirting forming permanent support, or deep concrete foundations. Inspectors take a strict, literal approach — if the method of joining resembles building works rather than mechanical connection, it fails. The classic failures? The twin-unit delivered in halves that then needs internal walls erected, structural timber to join the roof sections, or concrete pads poured specifically for it. Structures like that are routinely found to be operational development.

Single units raise different issues from twins. Delivered complete, they usually turn on mobility after installation — a single unit often fails because the installation has become too permanent. Twin units generate most of the appeals, because they depend on section thirteen, and their installation is where authorities allege building operations. Non-permanent support systems — jack legs, proprietary pad systems — are not foundations, and they're not a structural third section. The distinction between those and poured bases is frequently the whole case.

title: When a caravan is development
paras: So when is a caravan development? Section fifty-five of the T-C-P-A 1990 defines development in two limbs — operational development, and material change of use — and they supply the two questions every caravan case must answer. Is the caravan a building, or installed by building operations? And does its use amount to a material change of use of the land? A structure can meet every limb of the statutory caravan definition and still be development by either route.

The Measor case, in 1998, established that a structure satisfying the caravan definition will usually not be a building, because it lacks the necessary permanence — caravans rest on temporary supports and are movable without demolition. But Measor did not say a caravan can never be a building. The Carter and Byrne line of cases asks whether the installation amounts to building operations: deep or continuous foundations, anchors fixed into the ground, structural brick skirting, permanent buried services incompatible with removal, structurally integrated porches, decking or extensions — or a structure removable only by dismantling that goes beyond the simple separation section thirteen permits. The permanence analysis draws on Barvis and Elitestone: size, permanence, physical attachment.

Even an undisputed caravan's use may be development. A new planning unit forms where the caravan acquires its own curtilage, parking or access and operates as an independent household. Residential use commencing on agricultural land is almost always a material change. And intensification — more units, or year-round occupation of a seasonal site — can change the character of the use even where each unit is individually lawful.

The recent appeal record shows a disciplined, statute-led Inspectorate. A twin-unit in a garden — bolted together, on jacks, with reversible services and integrated ancillary occupation — was held to be a caravan whose siting was not development. The lack of any intention to move it was irrelevant. A visually similar garden "annexe", built in situ on poured concrete pads, with an integral sub-frame and fixed steps, failed on mobility and was a building erected by operational development — the neighbours' construction photographs defeating the applicant's generic factory images. And an enforcement notice alleging "residential use of the land" was varied, because the correct breach was the material change of use to the stationing of a caravan for residential purposes. Same subject matter, opposite outcomes — decided entirely on installation facts and precise analysis.

title: Ancillary caravan or independent dwelling
paras: Ancillary caravan, or independent dwelling? Start with curtilage. Curtilage is a factual assessment, not a legal fiction: the land immediately surrounding a dwelling, used for purposes intimately associated with its enjoyment. It's judged by physical layout, functional association, patterns of use and enclosure — and Land Registry boundaries are irrelevant. A caravan within the curtilage may fall within the same planning unit. One stationed in the paddock or field beyond it almost always creates a new use of land when it's occupied residentially. And be precise with the terms. Incidental use — a home office, hobby space, occasional guest sleeping — and ancillary use — living accommodation that supports and forms part of the main dwelling's residential function — are distinct, and both differ from independence.

The planning unit test derives from Burdle: the unit of occupation, and the purpose for which the land is used. No new unit forms where occupation is part of the same household, domestic life is integrated, no area is fenced off or treated separately, and no independent access or servicing exists. A separate unit — and with it, development — forms where occupation becomes autonomous: its own access, garden, parking, postal identity, separate metering, a distinct domestic life. And here is the sharpest single question: would the caravan continue to function as a home if the main dwelling were removed?

The contrast decides most cases. On one side, the twin-unit on jacks, connected by flexible services, the garden unsubdivided, the elderly relative sharing meals and domestic life with the household. That's within curtilage, integrated — no new planning unit, no development. On the other, the "garden lodge" with its own fenced area, decking, separate facilities, post delivered to the door, and an independent domestic life. Structurally still a caravan — but in land-use terms, a new dwellinghouse requiring permission. Facilities do not determine independence; patterns of occupation do. And inspectors say so consistently.

Temporary siting during a self-build is the recurring special case. The touring caravan is almost certainly lawful as a structure. The question is whether occupation is genuinely temporary and linked to the building operations, or has drifted into a new residential use of the land. Minimal supports, movement when construction requires it, and removal at completion point one way. Multi-year settled family occupation points the other. Staff accommodation on commercial, equestrian or agricultural land is the same analysis in a different setting — the stationing is a change of use of land needing its own justification, however lawful the business.

title: The 28-day rule and PD routes
paras: Let's turn to the twenty-eight-day rule and the permitted development routes. Two first principles govern every P-D argument in caravan cases. Permitted development rights apply only to development under section fifty-five — if the siting is not development, because it's a caravan, an ancillary use, the same planning unit, then P-D is irrelevant. The siting is already lawful. And P-D does not override the site-licensing regime or the Caravan Acts analysis. It's engaged only where the siting is development and the applicant relies on the General Permitted Development Order — the G-P-D-O — to avoid express permission.

Part 4, Class B permits the use of land for any purpose for not more than twenty-eight days in total in any calendar year. That's aggregate, not consecutive — and it's a right over the use of the land, not the use of the caravan. It legitimately covers temporary events, seasonal activities and occasional touring visitors. It does not permit operational development. It does not create an entitlement to live in a caravan for twenty-eight days. And it cannot be stretched into quasi-permanent residential occupation by rolling declarations, or by rotating units through the same pitch. Inspectors reject those claims consistently.

Part 4, Class A permits buildings and moveable structures — and a caravan can be one — required temporarily in connection with, and for the duration of, operations being carried out on that or adjoining land. The caravan must be genuinely required by the works: a site office, a welfare unit, security or supervision accommodation. And the right falls away when the works finish. A caravan used as the family home throughout a two-year self-build generally does not qualify, because the use is residential rather than construction-related. Authorities should ask for the building contract, the programme, the purpose of the caravan, and the intended duration.

For caravans specifically, the more precise route is Part 5, not Part 4. Part 5, Class A grants permitted development for the use of land as a caravan site in the licence-exempt circumstances set out in Schedule 1 to the Caravan Sites and Control of Development Act 1960. Paragraph 9 of that Schedule covers building and engineering sites. A caravan used for the accommodation of a person employed in connection with the operations on that or adjoining land needs neither a site licence nor express planning permission for the caravan-site use, for the duration of the works. Now, "employed" is undefined in the Act, and it's read fairly liberally as to who qualifies. But the accommodation must be genuinely tied to the operations, and the right falls away — with the caravans removed — when the works cease. This is the caravan-specific mechanism, and it's more robust than stretching the Part 4 temporary-structure right to cover what is really residential occupation.

Part 6 agricultural rights do not authorise residential use of caravans, and they do not authorise static caravans as workers' dwellings. At most, in narrow circumstances, they allow a caravan as a temporary agricultural structure — not habitation. And no P-D route for temporary use authorises the operational development that so often accompanies caravans: bases, foundations, engineered drainage, roads, hardstanding, structural decking — all of it needs its own justification. Nor does P-D ever permit a material change of use to a new dwellinghouse, or the formation of a separate planning unit.

title: LDCs, enforcement and the myths
paras: Finally — lawful development certificates, enforcement, and the myths. L-D-Cs are uniquely important here, because appearance is misleading, the decisive questions are questions of fact, and applicants need certainty against enforcement arriving years later. A section one-nine-one certificate — for existing lawfulness — is proved on the balance of probabilities: the exact physical characteristics, the installation method at the time of siting, the pattern and nature of the use — ancillary or independent — the duration where immunity is claimed, the planning unit, and the absence of building operations. It succeeds or fails on evidence, not planning merits.

A section one-nine-two certificate — for a proposed development — is the forward-looking route: the proposed twin-unit within curtilage, the like-for-like replacement caravan, the self-build arrangement. Its power is that method statements, engineering drawings and mobility evidence can be assessed before anything is sited — buying certainty. The predictable refusal errors? Rejecting because the unit "might" be lived in independently. Treating bedrooms and bathrooms as inherently creating a dwelling. Misunderstanding the final act of assembly. And conflating site licensing with planning.

Enforcement must target the correct breach — that's the golden rule, and mischaracterisation is the single biggest cause of failure at appeal. Allege a material change of use of the land to the stationing of a caravan or mobile home for residential purposes where occupation is independent, where residential use has commenced on agricultural land, or where numbers have intensified the use. Allege the erection of a building, or building operations comprising the installation, where foundations, integral sub-frames, structural skirting or attached works have converted siting into construction. And where the caravan is lawful but the works beneath it are not — hardstanding, bases, drainage — a split notice, requiring removal of the works while allowing the caravan, achieves the objective without overreach.

Route and fee follow the same analysis. If the structure is a caravan and the use is ancillary, the L-D-C is the cleanest and cheapest route. It removes planning judgment and policy debate, and it provides enduring certainty. If any element is development — oversize, building-level assembly, foundations, an independent unit — the proposal needs a full application at the full fee. And misclassification stores up validation problems, and problems with the Community Infrastructure Levy — C-I-L. A genuine caravan attracts no C-I-L. But a structure that is in law a building, or a use creating a dwellinghouse, engages it. Fee strategy is part of the legal assessment — not a late procedural step.
